data.title: Tapasya Investment Fund I: Bi-Annual Letter 2025
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Tapasya Investment Partners as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

It's approaching three years since the fund's inception, and I'm very pleased with our collective progress. This journey has allowed me to connect with many new individuals, a significant number of whom have become investors.

Thank you deeply for entrusting me with your capital and your time. As the fund grows through both strong performance and the addition of new investors, I will always value the crucial role each of you has played in its success.

While we underperformed the market in 2024, our fund has since outperformed it (as it did in 2022 and 2023), and I am satisfied with our year-to-date results. However, it's important to remember that short-term performance doesn't tell the whole story, and true outperformance is judged over longer periods.

This year has been marked by significant news, particularly regarding tariffs, which has contributed to considerable market volatility. We experienced a rapid correction in April, followed by a historically strong market rebound.

We capitalized on some of this volatility by increasing our existing positions and initiating a new one in Interactive Brokers (IBKR). Looking back, one area where I could have improved is being more decisive. The April volatility presented several opportunities in excellent companies. Due to a combination of limited deployable capital and a degree of risk aversion, I acted less decisively than I would have preferred in hindsight.

Despite this, I am happy that we have generated a substantial lead over the S&P 500 at both gross and net (LP) levels, and I am optimistic about maintaining similar outperformance for the remainder of the year.

2025 in Review - Mid Year

At the start of the year, I expressed concerns about high equity valuations in the US, noting limited opportunities and highlighting more promising prospects abroad, particularly in Europe and China. I also saw potential in homebuilders and small-cap companies at that time.

Since my January letter, the market landscape has shifted significantly, notably with the "Liberation Day" announcement on April 2nd. This event triggered a sharp but brief market correction, which allowed us to increase our stakes in some existing holdings and initiate a new position in Interactive Brokers (IBKR).

Another change this year involves how we manage our cash. Historically, holding cash has sometimes hindered our overall returns. To address this, I decided to allocate the cash portion to a broad-based index instead. We began by holding the S&P 500 through February, later switching to KWEB, a Chinese internet stock index. This strategy aims to improve returns. In the past, our typical 5% cash allocation, intended for capitalizing on short-term market fluctuations, occasionally dampened our results. To mitigate this, we now hold this cash in a broad-based index. The potential drawback of missing out on market fluctuations is now managed through access to a line of credit.

The anticipated recovery in the homebuilder sector has not yet materialized due to persistent high interest rates. This crucial segment of the economy has been facing a recession or significant slowdown for some time due to these elevated rates and high home prices.

Our current holdings in this sector include Builder FirstSource (BLDR, -10.7% YTD), Howard Hughes (HHH, -11.7% YTD), and Fannie Mae (+173.8% YTD), collectively representing about 12% of our portfolio. I maintain a long-term positive outlook for the homebuilding industry and intend to remain invested in BLDR and likely HHH. Our investment in FNMA, however, had a shorter-term objective related to its potential reclassification from a Government Sponsored Enterprise (GSE), which could lead to substantial gains. Following positive indications, we sold approximately half of our FNMA position at roughly over four times our initial purchase price.

Over the coming months, I anticipate a moderate decrease in interest rates, assuming government spending is reasonably controlled relative to revenue. Failure to achieve this could lead to broader economic challenges, impacting not only the housing industry.

We have exited our position in Topgolf Callaway (MODG). This was part of our offense portfolio, characterized by smaller, higher-risk bets with multi-bagger potential. This investment did not perform as expected, and we sold at a loss.

The initial investment rationale was based on the potential separation of the Topgolf and Callaway businesses, following Callaway's acquisition of Topgolf in 2021. However, given the current macroeconomic conditions and management's commentary on the spinoff, the original investment thesis no longer appeared sound, leading to our decision to sell.

Below are some of the best and worst performers for this year thus far.

Top Performers and Their Contribution

Our top contributors to our performance were Prosus, Uber and Carvana. On the other hand, our bottom performers were Lululemon, Builder FirstSource and Alphabet.

A few lines in honor (or shame) for the companies that made this list.

Prosus (PRX.AS): A core, long-term holding chosen for its significant stake in Tencent. The total market value of Prosus is currently less than its Tencent holding. We believe Tencent possesses substantial growth potential and a robust global competitive advantage. Additionally, Prosus holds approximately $35 billion in other listed and unlisted assets.

UBER (UBER): Our 2022 investment thesis, centered on a confluence of positive factors driving exceptional success (the "lalapalooza effect"), has materialized as anticipated. Uber continues to execute effectively and possesses a considerable growth trajectory, leading to its long-term holding status.

Carvana (CVNA): Carvana consistently surpasses execution expectations, gaining market share and achieving profitable revenue growth. Our initial investment thesis for Carvana is also proving accurate.

Lululemon (LULU): A relatively recent addition, Lululemon is viewed as a long-term compounder. We initiated our position during a market correction related to merchandise execution challenges. Current headwinds appear to be tariff-related and are expected to be temporary (under one year). We anticipate the company's strong brand and international expansion will drive positive results.

Alphabet (Google): Alphabet presents a nuanced outlook, facing potential competition in its core search business from emerging AI technologies like Open AI's Chat GPT and Perplexity. Drawing on my marketing experience and understanding of Google's competitive advantages, a cautious yet optimistic stance is warranted, as it is too early to draw definitive conclusions about long-term search dominance. Conversely, Google's cloud business is experiencing rapid and profitable growth, bolstering its overall position. Furthermore, Waymo's progress is noteworthy, now exceeding 250,000 rides per week. Overall, we are bullish on Alphabet while closely monitoring developments in the search landscape.

Builder First Source (BLDR): Builder First Source, a manufacturer and supplier of building materials, exhibits strong capital allocation and a return on invested capital exceeding 20%. The company generates significant free cash flow, which is strategically deployed for acquisitions in a fragmented market and substantial stock repurchases (a third of shares outstanding have been retired). This is considered a long-term holding. While the housing market is currently in a recession, we anticipate strong performance upon industry recovery. In the interim, the company's execution remains reasonable.

Our ability to stay largely indifferent to mark-to-market movements in our portfolio companies when they are not reflective of underlying business issues, remains one of our competitive advantages. Our ability to withstand volatility comes from our deep due diligence and understanding of companies we own, the collective patience of our investors and our dispassionate, rational approach to investing.

Many of the largest hedge funds’ competitive advantages come from massive technology and infrastructure investments, large-scale organizations, access to information seconds and even milliseconds before others, and extraordinary trading capabilities. None of these factors is present or relevant in our approach to investing. Importantly, we believe our competitive advantages are more durable than investment strategies that rely on these attributes.

This year, we introduced 1 new positions to our portfolio: Interactive Brokers (IBKR) which I am very pleased I was able to do that. It's been a company that I was never able to buy into, to my detriment, due to valuations. During the drawdown we had this year, the stock did get cheap and allowed us to build a position. I expect to hold this for the long term. You can also find my thesis for the company in the Global Investment Review X .

As we conclude this letter, we continue to operate in one of the most complex macroeconomic conditions in recent history. While inflation levels continue to moderate, they remain above central bank targets. The prolonged tightening cycle has caused shifting expectations around rate cuts, which has created a complicated environment for markets.

data.title: Disney Q4 Earnings: Some Concerning Trends Are Emerging
data.description:
After it became clear that The Walt Disney Company's (DIS)(DIS:CA) exciting narrative around streaming has fizzled out when the company reported Q3 2025 earnings, today the company reported the last quarter for the fiscal year, and the stock is now down by almost 6% since the last report.

In the meantime, as we could see on the graph below, the S&P 500 has continued its ascend, and with that, the negative performance gap between DIS and the market is now approaching 12% in just 3 months.

On the surface the report was not quite bad, but it seems that the optimism around the stock has driven it to unsustainable levels in the short-term. Earnings on a Non-GAAP basis came-in above the analysts' estimates, but the revenue miss was noteworthy.

When looking at the results on a segmented basis, it appears that the Entertainment segment is responsible for the daily drop in the share price. Revenue within the sector was down by 6%, which is not something that investors would expect, as Disney is looking to expand profitability in streaming.

Revenue dipped 0.5% year-over-year, including gains across the Sports (+2%) and Experiences (+6%) segments, while the Entertainment segment saw revenue decline 6%.

Source: Seeking Alpha

There were, however, other moving parts, and the investors' reaction to the quarter opens up the question of whether or not current expectations are too high to justify the share price.

A Sobering Quarter

Overall, there was nothing exciting about the fourth quarter, and the sharp drop confirms that investors were betting on a sustained turnaround. Revenue for the quarter was flat year-on-year, which was not something that believers of Disney+ success were expecting.

At the very top of the earnings press release, DIS management stressed the fact that "total segment operating income" was up by 12%, which would seem like a positive development for anyone interested in the company's operating performance. However, the term "total segment operating income" is actually a Non-GAAP measure that adjusts Disney's income before income taxes with a number of items (see them in blue below). Moreover, even the adjusted operating income measure fell by 5% in the last quarter.

More importantly, however, there are some concerning trends emerging on a segmented basis. Starting with the most important segment for future growth and profitability improvement - streaming. By simply looking at the year-on-year change in revenue and profits, one could assume that margin expansion is ongoing, and with topline growth of 8%, this could have a notable double whammy effect on the bottom line.

Underneath the surface, the main factor behind the operating income increase was the higher average revenue per user, coupled with fixed cost economies of scale due to the 5% growth in subscribers. However, the vast majority of the ARPU increase occurred at Disney+ International, while Domestic roughly kept up with inflation over the period.

When we plot the quarterly changes of ARPU in both of Disney+ segments, it becomes clear that ARPU in Domestic has stayed flat for the most part of the past 12 months.

Moreover, the lower U.S. dollar has been a tailwind of the International segment which is worth considering as Disney+ now derives more sales from overseas.

By far the most concerning part, however, is the fact that the DTC margin seems to be settling at around 5%. The hype from the first profitable quarter of Q2 2024 and the initial sharp increase in margins is now over, and there is now a risk that Disney+ might not be as profitable as initially anticipated.

Bear in mind that this segment was anticipated to take over from Disney's highly profitable legacy business, and now the probability of DTC margins settling at mid-single digits appears as a very risky outcome for Disney's shareholders.

The earnings release also portrayed results in the Experiences segment as a massive success with record operating income for both the fiscal year and the last 3-month period.

Experiences: Record full year segment operating income of $10.0 billion, an increase of $723 million compared to the prior year. Record Q4 segment operating income of $1.9 billion, an increase of $219 million compared to the prior-year quarter.

Source: Disney Q4 2025 Earnings Release

When it comes to actual margins, however, the segment's performance has been relatively stable over the past two years, with quarterly margins in line with those reported in FY 2023.

In terms of growth, the optimism through the first nine months of FY 2025 has now suddenly come to a halt, with year-on-year revenue growth down to around 6%.

Disney Stock Is Not Cheap

Similarly to the Q4 2025 results, Disney's stock might appear cheap when looking at certain valuation metrics in isolation. The Non-GAAP earnings multiple, for example, appears relatively low in comparison to the P/E multiple of the S&P 500, which is now above 30.

Investors could easily be led to the wrong conclusion by looking at the multiple on a historical basis. Although the multiple below is not adjusted for Non-GAAP items, it is now much closer to what DIS was trading prior to 2019 - roughly when problems within the company began surfacing.

This might seem like a reason enough to assume that DIS is at least reasonably priced at the moment and that the market might be overreacting to what was a somewhat mixed quarter.

The main issue, however, is that Disney is no longer the company that it was prior to 2019. At the very least, the all-in bet on streaming is transforming the business, and a simple comparison of valuation multiples on a historical basis is not enough.

A good example of this is to compare the price/sales multiple and the operating income margin over time. As we could see on the graph below, Disney's sales multiple has been moving roughly in-line with the margin increases from the past few years, but the stock now trades at roughly the same sales multiple as it did all the way back in FY 2012, when margins were significantly higher. And as growth remains low, it becomes harder to justify the current multiple by anything other than the fact that the overall equity market is richly priced.

Even though peers like Netflix (NFLX) have problems of their own, Disney is no longer seen as one of the most profitable entities in the Media space. Having operating margins of around 15% is now twice as low as those of the leader in streaming - Netflix.

Lastly, there's the dividend, which some might consider an indication that things are moving in the right direction. However, since the dividend was reintroduced, the stock's yield has been way below what it used to be before the company went all-in on streaming.

Given the fact that DIS now has an annual cash outflow of around $1.8bn in dividends, it is highly likely that we will continue to see dividend increases in the coming years. The reason why this might be shrugged off by the market is because the current dividend would need to increase by at least 50% in order for the yield to approach the normal pre-2019 levels.

Conclusion

When looking at the current earnings release in further detail, we could understand why the market is reacting so negatively to the results. There are some concerning trends emerging within Disney's streaming segment, and the stock does not reflect that. Quite the opposite, the company would have to continue to deliver on the margin and dividend front for a couple more years before one could justify DIS stock as a buy.

data.title: 4 Stocks That Capitalized On The Accelerating Adoption Of AI Infrastructure
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Calamos Timpani Small Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

Performance Review

For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, Calamos Timpani Small Cap Growth Fund returned 13.31% (ClassI shares at NAV) versus the Russell 2000 Growth Index return of 12.19%.

Positive Influences on Performance

Information Technology. The fund benefited from favorable security selection and an average overweight position in information technology. Positions in the communications equipment industry and the electronic equipment & instruments industry notably lifted relative performance.

Health Care. Favorable security selection and an average underweight allocation in health care added to returns, especially in the pharmaceuticals and health care supplies industries.

Negative Influences on Performance

Financials. Security selection within the financials sector dampened relative results, as holdings in the consumer finance and transaction & payment processing services industries lost ground.Industrials. Security selection and an average underweight stance in industrials, specifically in the heavy electrical equipment and aerospace & defense industries, lagged on a relative basis.

Fund Holdings - Contributors

Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE) 3.7% of fund as of 9/30/25

Profile: Lumentum Holdings Inc. is a leading designer and manufacturer of optical and photonic products used in telecommunications, data centers, industrial manufacturing, and consumer electronics.

Founded in 2015 and headquartered in San Jose, California, Lumentum is categorized as a communication equipment company in the information technology sector.

Analysis: Lumentum’s stock advanced 71% during the quarter, owing to a positive combination of strong financial performance, strategic growth initiatives, and favorable market dynamics. The company reported better-than-expected earnings, with revenue and earnings exceeding analyst forecasts, mainly driven by robust demand in its cloud and networking segment. Lumentum capitalized on the accelerating adoption of AI infrastructure, with record shipments of key optical components and the launch of innovative products like the R300 optical circuit switch. Strategic acquisitions and manufacturing expansion further bolstered investor confidence, while bullish analyst sentiment and technical momentum contributed to a notable rise in stock price. Broader market tailwinds and increased institutional interest also enhanced the stock’s performance during the quarter.

Rush Street Interactive Inc. (RSI) 3.1% of fund as of 9/30/25

Profile: Rush Street Interactive Inc. (RSI) is a prominent online gaming and sports betting company operating across the United States, Canada, and Latin America. The company is known for its flagship brands BetRivers, PlaySugarHouse, and RushBet, which offer real-money online casino games, sports betting, and social gaming platforms. Founded in 2012, the company is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois, and is categorized under casinos & gaming within the consumer discretionary sector.

Analysis: Rush Street Interactive’s common stock return was up 37%, driven by a combination of record- breaking financial performance, expanding user engagement, and strategic market growth. The company reported its ninth consecutive quarter of revenue and adjusted EBITDA growth, with Q2 revenue reaching $269 million—up 22% year-over-year—and adjusted EBITDA soaring 88% to over $40 million. Growth was fueled by strong performance in both online casino and sports betting segments, particularly in North America and Latin America, where monthly active users rose significantly. RSI also executed a $2.5 million stock buyback, signaling confidence in its valuation. Despite headwinds from increased taxes in Illinois, New Jersey, and Colombia, the company raised its full-year guidance, reflecting optimism about sustained momentum. Analyst upgrades and bullish sentiment further supported the stock’s 37% gain over the quarter.

Fund Holdings - Detractors

Dave Inc. (DAVE) 2.9% of fund as of 9/30/25

Profile: Dave Inc. takes its name from the “David vs Goliath” metaphor where Dave is a small, technology-focused, low-fee bank and Goliath represents the traditional, high fee big banks. Dave is headquartered in Los Angeles, California, and is listed as a consumer finance company within the financials sector. Dave was founded in 2015 and seeks to help consumers manage their finances through a variety of budgeting, liquidity, and banking services.

Analysis: Dave’s common stock declined 26%, driven by a mix of investor concerns and market reactions despite strong financial performance. Although the company reported impressive second-quarter earnings which beat analyst expectations, investors were unsettled by a rise in the 28-day delinquency rate to 2.40%, up from 2.03% the previous year. This uptick in credit risk, contributed to a sell-off after strong stock performance over the first half of 2025. Interestingly, Dave was one of our top contributors last quarter and remains in the top three among relative contributors year-to-date.

Bloom Energy Corp. (BE) (not owned)

Profile: Bloom Energy Corp. is a clean energy technology company that designs and manufactures solid oxide fuel cell systems for on-site power generation. Its flagship product, the Bloom Energy Server, converts fuels like natural gas, biogas, and hydrogen into electricity through an efficient electrochemical process without combustion. The company also offers hydrogen production solutions and serves customers in healthcare, data centers, manufacturing, and utilities with reliable, low-emission energy technologies. Incorporated in 2001, Bloom Energy is headquartered in San Jose, California, and is categorized as heavy electrical equipment within the industrials sector.

Analysis: We owned Bloom Energy early in 2025 but sold out of it as the stock struggled after the April tariff/liberation day market sell-off, and that decision looked smart through the end of 2Q, but Bloom Energy’s stock soared 254% during 3Q, driven by surging demand for its fuel cell technology to power AI-focused data centers, bolstered by a major partnership with Oracle (ORCL). Additionally, bullish analyst upgrades and price target hikes—some more than doubling prior estimates—fueled investor enthusiasm, while strong quarterly earnings and revenue growth reinforced confidence in the company’s operational momentum. Although we regrettably did not own Bloom Energy during the quarter, our other industrial sector holdings performed quite well. The relative drag from BE was the second largest individual stock for the quarter and surpassed the total relative drag from the industrials sector for the portfolio.

For additional information or to download a fact sheet, please visit the fund’s profile page: CTSIX - Timpani Small Cap Growth Mutual Fund | Calamos Investments

data.title: Calamos Timpani Small Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Summary

  • During the third quarter, small caps trounced large caps, and microcaps set the pace to beat.
  • Supported by Q3 gains, Calamos Timpani Small Cap Growth Fund (CTSIX) and Calamos Timpani SMID Growth Fund (CTIGX) remain well ahead of their benchmarks and peer averages for the year-to- date.
  • We are optimistic about the funds’ prospects—our fundamental-momentum investment style has been increasingly embraced by the market, our security selection within our framework has proven advantageous, and our nimble asset base has provided the flexibility to invest meaningfully in microcaps.
  • Sustained asset class rebounds would be the icing on the cake—nice to have but not required.

Investment Manager Discussion

Strong earnings and a US Federal Reserve interest rate cut fueled healthy equity performance during the third quarter. Even September returns were positive despite consensus concerns relating to typical seasonal weakness. Although large caps still lead small caps year-to-date by 421 basis points (as 1 measured by the Russell 2000 Index versus the Russell 1000 Index), small caps outperformed for the quarter, rising an impressive 12.39% and exceeding large-cap performance by 440 basis points.

So what’s been driving our strong performance? It’s been a combination of our investment style being embraced by the market (our focus on fundamental momentum—sustainably fast and underestimated growth) and successful stock picking within that framework, including our exposure to microcaps.

As our performance has shown, we don’t need asset class strength to put points on the board. However, asset class strength could provide another way for our funds to win. Asset allocators, I would pay attention.

Brandon Nelson, CFA SVP and Senior Portfolio Manager

Market Overview

The US equity markets continued to rebound from their April lows and generated strong gains in the third quarter, demonstrating remarkable resilience in the face of persistent uncertainties. While growth sectors led the recovery, gains were broad-based, as concerns about tariffs and economic conditions faded somewhat.

Strong earnings and a US Federal Reserve interest rate cut fueled healthy equity performance during the third quarter. Even September returns were positive despite consensus concerns relating to typical seasonal weakness. Although large caps still lead small caps year-to-date by 421 basis points (as measured by the Russell 2000 Index +10.39% versus the Russell 1000 Index +14.60%), small caps outperformed for the quarter, rising an impressive 12.39% and exceeding large-cap performance by 440 basis points.

Within the Russell 2000 Growth Index (+12.19%), materials (+25.2%), utilities (+20.5%), industrials (+20.4%), energy (+17.1%), and information technology (+14.0%) all outperformed. Health care (+11.3%), consumer discretionary (+7.8%), real estate (+6.2%), communication services (+1.9%), and financials (0.0%) lagged, and consumer staples (-0.3%) was negative in a strong, risk-on quarter.

Performance Review

For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, Calamos Timpani Small Cap Growth Fund returned 13.31% (ClassI shares at NAV) versus the Russell 2000 Growth Index return of 12.19%.

Positive Influences on Performance

Information Technology. The fund benefited from favorable security selection and an average overweight position in information technology. Positions in the communications equipment industry and the electronic equipment & instruments industry notably lifted relative performance.

Health Care. Favorable security selection and an average underweight allocation in health care added to returns, especially in the pharmaceuticals and health care supplies industries.

Negative Influences on Performance

Financials. Security selection within the financials sector dampened relative results, as holdings in the consumer finance and transaction & payment processing services industries lost ground.Industrials. Security selection and an average underweight stance in industrials, specifically in the heavy electrical equipment and aerospace & defense industries, lagged on a relative basis.

Fund Holdings - Contributors

Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE) 3.7% of fund as of 9/30/25

Profile: Lumentum Holdings Inc. is a leading designer and manufacturer of optical and photonic products used in telecommunications, data centers, industrial manufacturing, and consumer electronics.

Founded in 2015 and headquartered in San Jose, California, Lumentum is categorized as a communication equipment company in the information technology sector.

Analysis: Lumentum’s stock advanced 71% during the quarter, owing to a positive combination of strong financial performance, strategic growth initiatives, and favorable market dynamics. The company reported better-than-expected earnings, with revenue and earnings exceeding analyst forecasts, mainly driven by robust demand in its cloud and networking segment. Lumentum capitalized on the accelerating adoption of AI infrastructure, with record shipments of key optical components and the launch of innovative products like the R300 optical circuit switch. Strategic acquisitions and manufacturing expansion further bolstered investor confidence, while bullish analyst sentiment and technical momentum contributed to a notable rise in stock price. Broader market tailwinds and increased institutional interest also enhanced the stock’s performance during the quarter.

Rush Street Interactive Inc. (RSI) 3.1% of fund as of 9/30/25

Profile: Rush Street Interactive Inc. (RSI) is a prominent online gaming and sports betting company operating across the United States, Canada, and Latin America. The company is known for its flagship brands BetRivers, PlaySugarHouse, and RushBet, which offer real-money online casino games, sports betting, and social gaming platforms. Founded in 2012, the company is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois, and is categorized under casinos & gaming within the consumer discretionary sector.

Analysis: Rush Street Interactive’s common stock return was up 37%, driven by a combination of record- breaking financial performance, expanding user engagement, and strategic market growth. The company reported its ninth consecutive quarter of revenue and adjusted EBITDA growth, with Q2 revenue reaching $269 million—up 22% year-over-year—and adjusted EBITDA soaring 88% to over $40 million. Growth was fueled by strong performance in both online casino and sports betting segments, particularly in North America and Latin America, where monthly active users rose significantly. RSI also executed a $2.5 million stock buyback, signaling confidence in its valuation. Despite headwinds from increased taxes in Illinois, New Jersey, and Colombia, the company raised its full-year guidance, reflecting optimism about sustained momentum. Analyst upgrades and bullish sentiment further supported the stock’s 37% gain over the quarter.

Fund Holdings - Detractors

Dave Inc. (DAVE) 2.9% of fund as of 9/30/25

Profile: Dave Inc. takes its name from the “David vs Goliath” metaphor where Dave is a small, technology-focused, low-fee bank and Goliath represents the traditional, high fee big banks. Dave is headquartered in Los Angeles, California, and is listed as a consumer finance company within the financials sector. Dave was founded in 2015 and seeks to help consumers manage their finances through a variety of budgeting, liquidity, and banking services.

Analysis: Dave’s common stock declined 26%, driven by a mix of investor concerns and market reactions despite strong financial performance. Although the company reported impressive second-quarter earnings which beat analyst expectations, investors were unsettled by a rise in the 28-day delinquency rate to 2.40%, up from 2.03% the previous year. This uptick in credit risk, contributed to a sell-off after strong stock performance over the first half of 2025. Interestingly, Dave was one of our top contributors last quarter and remains in the top three among relative contributors year-to-date.

Bloom Energy Corp. (BE) (not owned)

Profile: Bloom Energy Corp. is a clean energy technology company that designs and manufactures solid oxide fuel cell systems for on-site power generation. Its flagship product, the Bloom Energy Server, converts fuels like natural gas, biogas, and hydrogen into electricity through an efficient electrochemical process without combustion. The company also offers hydrogen production solutions and serves customers in healthcare, data centers, manufacturing, and utilities with reliable, low-emission energy technologies. Incorporated in 2001, Bloom Energy is headquartered in San Jose, California, and is categorized as heavy electrical equipment within the industrials sector.

Analysis: We owned Bloom Energy early in 2025 but sold out of it as the stock struggled after the April tariff/liberation day market sell-off, and that decision looked smart through the end of 2Q, but Bloom Energy’s stock soared 254% during 3Q, driven by surging demand for its fuel cell technology to power AI-focused data centers, bolstered by a major partnership with Oracle (ORCL). Additionally, bullish analyst upgrades and price target hikes—some more than doubling prior estimates—fueled investor enthusiasm, while strong quarterly earnings and revenue growth reinforced confidence in the company’s operational momentum. Although we regrettably did not own Bloom Energy during the quarter, our other industrial sector holdings performed quite well. The relative drag from BE was the second largest individual stock for the quarter and surpassed the total relative drag from the industrials sector for the portfolio.

Positioning and Portfolio Changes

From a sector standpoint, the portfolio’s largest weights reside within information technology and health care on an absolute basis, while the smallest sector weights with holdings are in energy and communication services. The portfolio had no exposure to the utilities and real estate sectors. We maintain overweight allocations to the casinos & gaming and communications equipment industries. The largest industry underweight positions are biotechnology and application software.

Allocations to industrials and information technology rose during the period with increased weights in trading companies & distributors and electronic equipment & instruments. By contrast, allocations to consumer discretionary and health care decreased over the period with reductions to education services and biotechnology.

data.title: DBC: Why Commodities May Be The Next Big Trade (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Commodities are flying under the radar, but their uptrend has been strong since the middle of 2023. Back then, the AI trade was really gaining steam (recall NVIDIA's (NVDA) gangbuster Q1 earnings report that year), while consumers were thankful for a sharp drop in oil and other "real" asset prices.

Today, I'm revisiting the Invesco DB Commodity Index Tracking Fund (DBC). The ETF tracks a basket of global commodities, and I had a cautious hold rating on it back in November of 2023. Over the past two years, DBC has gained just 5%, underperforming the S&P 500 by almost 50 percentage points.

I am upgrading it to a buy today.

Commodity Index: Fresh Multi-Year Highs

According to the issuer, DBC "seeks to track changes in the DBIQ Optimum Yield Diversified Commodity Index Excess Return plus interest of the fund's holdings. The rules-based index is composed of futures contracts on 14 of the most heavily traded and important physical commodities in the world. The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted annually in November."

DBC is a rather small ETF with just $1.3 billion in assets under management as of November 11, 2025. That's down from $2.0 billion at the time of my previous analysis. The annual expense ratio is high at 85 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is also high at 4.79%.

Share-price momentum has been steady lately, earning the product a B- ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha's quantitative scoring system. In terms of risk, the historical standard deviation has not been all that lofty lately, but the fund is quite tied to cyclical trends in the global economy.

Finally, on liquidity, DBC scores well, given an average daily volume of more than 400,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of only four basis points, per Invesco ETFs.

So, what makes me bullish? Well, there are indications that US GDP growth could remain firm in the quarters ahead, a soft labor market notwithstanding. Prediction markets peg the Q4 expansion rate at 2%, despite the government shutdown that will have endured for about half of the period.

US GDP Growth Holding Firm

What's more, GDP could catch a boost from unusually large tax refunds early next year. Couple that with the chance of $2,000 stimulus checks being doled out to families making under $100,000, and that could add fuel to the inflation fire - commodity prices, specifically.

Big Tax Refunds Expected in 2026

It's important to know what's in the ETF, though. Energy commands a high weight. Oil and gasoline-related products must be watched carefully, and I acknowledge that a well-supplied global oil market is a bearish risk over the quarters to come.

Additionally, if the US jobs situation deteriorates further, then that would put more pressure on energy demand. Thus, it's not a clear sky for DBC, but with downbeat commodity sentiment and the space performing well during what's historically a bearish seasonal period, attention should be paid.

DBC: Exposure Details

DBC: Gains Have Come During A Usually-Bearish Calendar Period

But it's not just an energy story. Metals (both precious and industrial) are in general rally mode, while many "soft" commodities in the agricultural arena have been in pronounced bull markets. Coffee is perhaps the standout there, with futures prices going from under $90 in 2019 to above $400 during parts of 2025.

In a sense, long commodities are a play on climate change and the drastic impacts it can have on agriculture.

Coffee Futures Have Soared

Bigger picture and from a macro perspective, no commodity analysis is complete without a view of what's happening with the US Dollar Index (DXY).

I see resistance in the 100 to 100.60 area. A break above that would likely be bearish for DBC. A breakdown below the YTD low under 97 would lay the foundation for DBC to venture closer to its 2022 high above $27.

US Dollar Index: 100-100.60 Resistance, Down YTD and YoY

DBC: Rounded Bottom Forming, Solid Rally Since April

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on DBC. I see the bias to the upside on a broad basket of commodities. The asset area has been in a slow and steady uptrend, which has flown under the radar as the AI megatrend captures much of the media's attention.

Having bucked usual bearish seasonal trends and with a bullish calendar period on tap, allocating more toward resources could pay off.

data.title: U.S. Crude Oil Stocks Jump
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey Commodities Strategist

Oil prices managed to edge higher yesterday, despite several bearish data releases.

Energy- IEA continues to see very well-supplied oil market

Oil prices moved higher yesterday, settling just shy of 0.5% higher, despite a bearish weekly Energy Information Administration (EIA) inventory report. Also, a monthly International Energy Agency (IEA) release again highlighted expectations for a sizable oil surplus in 2026.

The EIA’s report showed that US crude oil inventories increased by 6.4m barrels over the last week, larger than expected and more than the 1.3m barrel increase the API reported the previous day. This leaves crude stocks at their highest level since June.

Seasonally, they are at their lowest level since 2014. The increase was largely driven by weaker exports, which declined by 1.55m b/d week on week. For refined products, gasoline and distillate stocks fell by 945k barrels and 637k barrels, respectively.

These inventory declines come despite refiners increasing utilisation rates by 3.4 percentage points, week on week, to 89.4%. Run rates are expected to increase as refinery maintenance concludes, while healthy refinery margins are likely to also support higher refinery run rates.

The IEA’s monthly report continues to indicate a well-supplied market. The agency estimates that global oil supply will grow by 3.1m b/d and 2.5m b/d in 2025 and 2026, respectively. Meanwhile, demand growth is forecast to be more modest, with the IEA expecting it to increase by just 790k per day (b/d) in 2025 and a further 770k b/d in 2026.

In terms of oil inventories, the IEA estimates that global observed stocks surged by 77.7m barrels in September, with a large increase in floating storage. Meanwhile, preliminary data shows that global stocks increased further in October, driven once again by floating storage.

While the ICE gasoil crack has fallen from its recent highs over the past couple of days, it remains at elevated levels, above $30/bbl. As we head deeper into the Northern Hemisphere winter, refinery maintenance season, a number of unplanned refinery outages, Russian sanction uncertainty, and low stocks have kept the middle distillate market well-supported.

The latest inventory data from Enterprise Singapore shows that onshore middle distillate stocks in the nation fell by 119k barrels over the last week. In the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp (ARA) region, gasoil stocks increased by 87kt WoW to 2.29mt, according to Insights Global.

data.title: BYD: Not To Be Confused With Legacy Autos
data.description:
Introduction

I initially covered BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF, OTCPK:BYDDY) in May of this year, citing its increasing market share in EVs as it expands across the world with exports or production facilities. However, the stock has declined since then due to mediocre results, pressured by competition in the core China market as well as higher working capital and lower than expected margins as it penetrates new markets. However, 2026 should see a more normal growth rate with a recovery in margins and cash flow. What is not priced into BYD is its autonomous vehicle technology, nor its humanoid robot production plans.

A Disappointing 2025

BYD started the year with massive growth and margins that quickly turned south as the Chinese market went into a price war, which may persist in 4Q25. There are over 100 EV manufacturers that should consolidate to 15 or 30 in the next five years. This incredible level of competition has sparked constant innovation in design, manufacturing, and technology. It is a true creative destruction scenario that is making the Chinese automakers the ones to fear as they begin expanding globally.

Robotaxi and Humanoid Robots

Autonomous vehicles (AV) and humanoid robot technology are advancing rapidly, especially in China. I covered XPeng (XPEV) last week, which seems to have a viable product. Pony AI (PONY) is another AV provider I have researched. Tesla (TSLA) is by no means alone in the US market and may face significant competition globally from Chinese makers.

BYD signed a 100k unit robotaxi JV with Uber, which is still in the launch phase, but given the technological advances at other companies, BYD may not be far behind and has the balance sheet to acquire them. It has also announced the launch of general-purpose humanoid robots for US$10k. These initiatives are not priced into the shares, unlike Tesla; BYD is still valued as an automaker.

Operating Model

Long-term consensus assumes 11%-unit sales growth, with hybrids maintaining a lead vs pure EVs. This is where the export market comes in, as the charging infrastructure is not built out, and it avoids range fear. Average prices are around US$20k, indicating a mass market focus and affordable to lower-income markets. EBIT margins are projected to rise to the 7% level vs the 5.3% drop seen in 2025. This is due to an assumption of reduced-price competition in China and scale gains from overseas manufacturing. BYD still has a sizable smartphone and electronics assembler business with clients such as Apple (AAPL) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), and that may grow over 15% on tight margins of under 4% that produce positive cash flow.

Financial Estimates

One of BYD's strengths is its size or scale, which allows it to generate positive free cash flow of over US$5bn and more so in lower capex cycles. The company has increased dividends, but I would assume that cash build-up would be used for M&A of other Chinese players or to accelerate the global expansion. European, Asian, and US automakers are susceptible given their higher costs and lower technology base. In many markets, it has taken over dormant factories to jump-start domestic manufacturing.

BYD should see cash earnings (net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation) rebound in 2026 vs a weak 2025 and then trend towards a 10% range, barring M&A, robotaxi, or robot initiatives.

Valuation

It's no secret that BYD is a cheap stock, but so are most automakers. The sector was de-rated some time ago with the EV transition, a slower global economy, and affordability issues. This is why the consensus price target for YE26 of US$18 is 9x P/CE (cash earnings) or 0.6x PEG. It's in line with or even above the sector (excluding Tesla). Which means that BYD will need to create a higher growth narrative, like Tesla or XPeng, to garner higher valuations.

I have cut my fair value PEG to 0.75x from 1x to incorporate this sector view, which still provides ample upside of 70%. However, there may be a high degree of frustration as the market lumps BYD along with the major legacy automakers that have far higher risk profiles in my view.

Peer Comps

As discussed above, the auto sector has been de-rated with many legacy companies trading under 0.5x PEG despite positive FCF and dividends. The market views this sector as one of high competition, capital-intensive, and susceptible to technological and regulatory challenges. Only Tesla escapes this top-down view as its growth story continues to promise untold riches. BYD and XPeng may break into this high-growth narrative and garner greater valuations.

Risk

This is a highly competitive market susceptible to margin-crushing price wars, exacerbated by macro downturns. BYD may repeat weak numbers of 2025 and face headwinds in its global expansion that may, in turn, damage its balance sheet.

Conclusion

In rate BYD a buy. Despite a weak 2025, I find that the company's scale, global reach, and yet unproven AV and robotics technology are compelling given the low valuations. It seems that the market is lumping BYD in with the other automakers that may be more challenged.

data.title: Impax High Yield Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

• The strategy outperformed its benchmark during the third quarter on a gross returns basis. Security selection contributed to relative performance while allocation decisions in a handful of sectors, notably the portfolio’s lack of energy sector exposure, detracted.

Market review

• Despite elevated geopolitical risk and ongoing concerns about US fiscal sustainability, risk assets rallied during Q3 and delivered positive returns as trade tensions subsided and investors turned their attention to the US Federal Reserve (Fed). The Fed met twice during the period, holding interest rates steady in July but cutting them by 25 basis points (BPS) in September, lowering the target range to 4.00% to 4.25%. The move was widely anticipated as a response to a softening labor market and viewed by many as an ‘insurance cut’. The Fed’s messaging remained balanced between inflation concerns and labor market weakness, though the updated ‘dot plot’ leaned slightly ‘dovish’, signalling two more rate cuts by year-end, albeit with a wide range of views among policymakers. Notably, the Fed also revised its economic growth outlook upward.

• The US Treasury yield curve steepened by 6 bps over the period, with yields on the 2- and 10-year segments declining by 11 and 7 basis points, respectively. Although the yield on 5-year Treasuries ended 6 basis points lower, at 3.74%, it experienced significant volatility during the period — initially rising by 24 bps, then falling by 48 bps, before climbing again. Despite the bouts of volatility, the spread-to-worst on the benchmark (ICE BofA BB-B US Cash Pay High Yield Constrained Index) ended the period 12 bps tighter, at 237 bps. The yield-to-worst on the benchmark also fell by 21 bps while the par-weighted coupon rose modestly by 1 bps, to end the period at 6.08% and 6.42%, respectively.

• During the period, performance across the US high yield market was driven by the lower-quality segments, as bonds rated ‘CCC’ and below outperformed ‘BB’s and Single-‘B’s; quarterly returns from these segments of the credit market were 3.99%, 2.39%, and 2.20%, respectively. From a sector perspective, energy, media and basic industry were the top contributors to market performance. Conversely, the banking, insurance and transportation sectors were the laggards, in relative terms, despite posting positive total returns over the quarter.

• The par-weighted high yield bond default rate, including distressed exchanges, fell by 2 bps to 1.39% and remains well below the 25-year average. Leveraged loan default rates also fell over the quarter by 30 bps to 3.49%, above the 25-year average of 3.1%.

• Over the third quarter, gross high yield bond issuance surged to US$122bn (and US$34bn net of maturities), marking the fifth most active quarter on record. This was driven primarily by refinancing activity. Fund flows into US high yield remained positive, with US$5.0bn of inflows — nearly double Q2’s $2.7bn — indicating strong investor demand. With supply turning positive and being well absorbed by investors, market technical factors remained supportive throughout the period.

Contributors

From a sector allocation perspective, the overweight positions in the Telecommunications and Media sectors, coupled with the underweight to the Leisure sector, positively contributed to performance. Security selection among the Technology & Electronics, Retail and Basic Industry sectors also helped relative performance, with standout contributors including Unisys, PetSmart and Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF). Unisys reported strong Q2 2025 results, exceeding expectations across revenue and earnings, while raising full-year guidance for operating margin and pre-pension free cash flow. Management expects several delayed signings to close during late 2025. PetSmart’s earnings have stabilized, with strong free cash flow and adequate liquidity supporting a steady leverage profile. The team remains comfortable with the overall risk profile and believes the bonds offer relative value. Cleveland-Cliffs bonds rallied in Q3 and spreads materially tightened, driven by strong Q2 earnings (EBITDA) growth, fixed contract extensions, and a surprise cost decline. Solid liquidity, no near-term maturities and disciplined capital spending are reinforcing its credit profile.

Detractors

The strategy’s lack of Energy exposure, cash drag in a risk-on environment and overweight allocations to the Technology & Electronics and Insurance sectors detracted from relative performance. Security selection was challenged within the Media, Capital Goods and Utility sectors. Within Media, relative performance was held back by staying underweight to Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). The group’s bonds were downgraded to high yield following the company’s planned split between Streaming & Studios and the weaker Global Networks segment. The company’s liability management exercise debt exchanges reduced bondholder protections and left unsecured debt with the declining Networks unit. The bonds rallied late in Q3 on low probability bid rumors. Within Capital Goods, Toucan TopCo announced modest revenue and earnings (EBITDA) declines for H1 and Q2 after weak volumes in select segments, lingering European cost inefficiencies and the exit of non-core businesses. While the bonds offer an attractive yield, recent performance headwinds warrant a more cautious stance. Meanwhile, within Utilities, bonds issued by XPLR Infrastructure (a subsidiary of NextEra Energy (NEE)) underperformed in Q3 largely due to strategic uncertainty and financial restructuring at its various project-level entities. Despite strong execution and a high-quality asset base, elevated leverage and reduced earnings visibility weighed on investor sentiment and credit outlook.

Notable changes

During the period, the investment team increased its overweight position in the Telecommunications sector. At the same time, it reduced underweight exposures to the Basic Industry and Retail sectors. Conversely, the strategy trimmed its overweight allocation to Healthcare, ending with a slight underweight position. The overweight allocation to the Services sector was also scaled back and managers deepened the underweight in Utilities. From a credit rating perspective, the strategy reduced its off-benchmark exposure to ‘BBB’-rated credits while increasing its exposure to off-benchmark ‘CCC’ and below rated bonds. The underweight allocation to ‘BB’s was also reduced as was the overweight exposure to Single-‘B’-rated credits.

Outlook

Credit spreads tightened modestly in Q3 and remain near record low levels, despite numerous economic uncertainties. The US economy continues to perform “well-enough” and capital markets remain very receptive to corporate debt issuance of almost any quality. This is a very supportive backdrop for the high yield market. The investment team will continue to actively participate in select new issues and remain fully invested. The strategy has not materially changed its overall risk profile, which is deliberately modest in recognition of the very low spread environment. It is possible that credit spreads remain very low for a prolonged period given the stability of the economy, minimal default activity and seemingly strong global demand for corporate bond exposure. Inflation data continues to moderate, and the Fed appears ready to counteract any material weakness in economic data should it arise. We remain selective and cautious with lower quality names at this point in the cycle, however. Our outlook remains balanced with continued uncertainty around tariff negotiations, federal budget concerns and geopolitical risks offset by the numerous strengths mentioned above. The investment team’s primary focus remains on careful credit selection and relatively neutral overall risk positioning. While spreads are low, the relatively high yields currently on offer should be the primary determinant of total returns in the asset class.

Fund overview

Impax High Yield Bond Fund is a diversified high yield strategy targeting high current income and capital appreciation that seeks to mitigate risks by incorporating sustainability analysis into credit research and selection.

data.title: Carillon Chartwell Small Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

U.S. stocks staged a powerful rally after the significant drawdown following President Donald Trump's tariff announcements in early April. The rebound continued in the third quarter, with notably strong performance from small-cap value stocks, which had previously lagged other segments, particularly large-cap growth.

This market rotation was likely driven by attractive relative valuations and the expectation of interest rate cuts from the U.S. Federal Reserve, which could disproportionately benefit smaller companies. Total return for the Russell 2000® Value Index, the Fund's benchmark, was 12.6% in the third quarter.

Unfortunately, much of the benchmark's gain was driven by a very narrow set of low-quality companies. High beta, high volatility, and high variability were rewarded while profitability and low valuations were penalized. Many of the strongest performers were unprofitable companies with unproven business models in fledgling industries such as cryptocurrency, nuclear fission, and quantum computing.

Most of the strongest performers have small weights in the Russell 2000 Value Index, but their collective impact was significant, particularly during a September short squeeze when less than 5% of the benchmark's constituents contributed 100% of its total return for the month. Most of the Fund's poor relative performance in the third quarter occurred during this period.

Fund Review

Top securities

Cushman & Wakefield (CWK), which provides commercial real estate services, reported better than expected earnings and revenue, driven by strong performance in its capital markets and leasing businesses.

Helios Technologies (HLIO) manufactures highly engineered motion control and electronic control technology. The company's share price received a boost after reporting that its margins had improved and signaling that its end markets may be stabilizing.

nVent (NVT) provides electrical connection and protection solutions that support critical infrastructure across industrial, commercial, and energy markets. Investors reacted favorably to the company's accelerating growth outlook, which was fueled by strong demand from data center and utility customers.

American Eagle (AEO), a specialty retailer focused on casual apparel and accessories for teens and young adults, rallied after new marketing campaigns drove better same-store sales.

TriMas (TRS) is a diversified manufacturer of packaging, aerospace components, and specialty products. Investors reacted favorably to the company's double-digit organic growth and margin expansion.

Bottom securities

Kemper (KMPR) is a property and casualty insurer focusing on non-standard personal lines of coverage distributed through independent agents. Shares lagged after the company reported elevated auto losses and issued a cautious outlook for the year.

Bruker (BRKR) is a global leader in scientific instruments and analytical solutions for the life sciences, pharmaceutical, and industrial research markets. The company's share price was pressured by dilution concerns connected to its announcement of a large convertible offering.

Progress Software (PRGS) allows customers to develop, deploy, and manage critical business applications. Investors were discouraged by the company's weak organic growth and margin pressure.

Nomad Foods (NOMD), a major European frozen food manufacturer, reported soft volume and margin trends amid ongoing demand and pricing challenges.

Six Flags (FUN) operates regional theme parks across North America. The company's share price was weak after it reported disappointing results and reduced guidance due to weather disruptions and pressure on low-income consumers.

Outlook

The recent outperformance of small-cap value stocks is a welcome change, but its exceptionally narrow, low-quality leadership has made it challenging for many active managers to keep pace with the Russell 2000® Value Index. On the other hand, many stocks that lagged the benchmark in the early stages of this rebound may offer substantial upside if the recovery broadens and investor interest in the segment persists.

Portfolio turnover has increased as we have attempted to take advantage of this performance bifurcation, and we believe many of the Fund's current holdings are significantly undervalued. We're optimistic that lower interest rates, coupled with the Trump administration's efforts to encourage domestic spending and investment, can boost profits and promote higher valuations for these companies. And if that scenario doesn't materialize, we believe their higher quality could be an important source of downside mitigation, which may be lacking from the speculative winners of the past few months.

We remain committed to our philosophy and process and continue to prioritize value, quality, and the potential for fundamental improvement when investing the Fund's capital. Its current positioning reflects this bottom-up process with modest overweights in the industrials, consumer staples, and information technology sectors, along with modest underweights in the financials, utilities, and communication services sectors.

The Fund may invest in ETFs and therefore would be subject to the same risks as the underlying securities in which the ETF invests as well as entails higher expenses than if invested into the underlying ETF directly.

Definitions

Artificial intelligence (AI) Technology that enables computers to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity and autonomy.

Beta A measure of the volatility or systemic risk of a security, group of securities, or portfolio compared with another security, group of securities, portfolio, or the market as a whole.

Convertible offering a security that can be converted into common stock at a later date.

Drawdown A decline in the returns of a security or group of securities, as measured over a period from the peak of returns to their trough.

Growth investing a stock-buying strategy that focuses on companies expected to grow at an above-average rate compared to their industry or the market.

Guidance Statements from managers of publicly traded companies about their expectations for near-term profits or losses.

Market capitalization, or market cap The total dollar market value of a company's outstanding shares of stock.

Narrow Describes the relationship between the median and the mean of a market index: When a few data outliers result in a mean that is substantially larger (or smaller) than the median of the full data set, then the performance of the entire index is being driven by a narrow selection of companies. An index supported by broad market movements is one where the median is closer to the mean.

Overweight A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Positioning Assessments of whether professional investors are, on the whole, bullish or bearish on a particular security, industry, sector, market capitalization or other area of the market, as reflected by the extent to which they are invested in the area of the market in question.

Quality investing Investment strategies that seek to invest in companies with low debt, stable earnings, consistent asset growth, and strong corporate governance, as reflected in metrics such as return to equity and debt to equity ratios, as well as to earnings variability.

Relative valuations Valuations calculated with similar and comparable assets to determine the value of the assets relative to each other.

Rotation the movement of investments in securities from one industry, sector, factor, or asset class to another as market participants react to or try to anticipate the next stage of the economic cycle.

Short squeeze When the price of an asset rises sharply, forcing short sellers to buy it to avoid incurring excessive losses. Those purchases put more upward pressure on the asset price.

Total return The actual rate of return of an investment or investments over a given period of time. Total return includes interest, capital gains, dividends, and distributions realized over the specified period.

Underweight A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Value investing Investment strategies that seek to invest in stocks that appear to be trading for less than their intrinsic or book value.

Indices

The Russell 2000® Value Index, the Fund's benchmark index, is an unmanaged index that measures the performance of those Russell 2000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values.
